```{r, echo=FALSE, results="hide"}
set.seed(sample.int(.Machine$integer.max, 1))

## Parametros
sigma <- sample(c(1, 2, 3), 1)
n     <- sample(c(10, 20, 30, 50, 100), 1)

## Razao t_{n-1, 0.975} / 1.96
correct <- round(qt(0.975, n - 1) / 1.96, 4)
w1 <- round(qt(0.975, n) / 1.96, 4)
w2 <- round(1.96 / qt(0.975, n - 1), 4)
w3 <- round(qt(0.975, n - 1), 4)
w4 <- round(qt(0.975, n - 1) - 1.96, 4)

all_vals <- c(correct, w1, w2, w3, w4)
# Garante cinco alternativas numericamente distintas após a parametrização.
integer_choices <- all(abs(all_vals - round(all_vals)) < sqrt(.Machine$double.eps))
choice_step <- if (integer_choices) 1 else max(1e-4, max(abs(all_vals), na.rm = TRUE) * 0.01)
while (anyDuplicated(all_vals)) {
  duplicate_position <- which(duplicated(all_vals))[1]
  all_vals[duplicate_position] <- max(all_vals, na.rm = TRUE) + choice_step
}
choices <- sample(all_vals)
sol_pos <- which(choices == correct)
sol_str <- paste(as.integer(seq_along(choices) == sol_pos), collapse = "")
```

Question
========
Para $X_i \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ com $\sigma^2$ desconhecido, o IC 95% baseado em $t_{n-1}$ tem comprimento $2\,t_{n-1,\,0{,}975}\,S/\sqrt{n}$, enquanto o IC baseado na normal (com $\sigma$ conhecido) teria comprimento $2 \times 1{,}96\,\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Com $n = `r n`$, a razao $t_{n-1,\,0{,}975}/1{,}96$ (que mede o quanto o IC $t$ e mais largo) e:

Answerlist
----------
* `r choices[1]`
* `r choices[2]`
* `r choices[3]`
* `r choices[4]`
* `r choices[5]`

Solution
========
$t_{`r n-1`,\,0{,}975}/1{,}96 = `r qt(0.975,n-1)`/1{,}96 = `r correct`$.

Para $n$ grande, $t_{n-1,\,0{,}975} \to 1{,}96$ e a razao tende a 1. Para $n = `r n`$, o IC $t$ e `r ifelse(correct > 1, paste0(round((correct-1)*100,1), "% mais largo"), "essencialmente igual ao IC normal")`.

Meta-information
================
exname: Cap5S2Q7_razao_t_vs_normal
extype: schoice
exsolution: `r sol_str`
exshuffle: FALSE
